title: Workbook — The Paschal Mystery
html:
Student Workbook

To use after class or in groups. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What does the word "Paschal" mean and where does it come from?
  2. Name the five moments of the Paschal Mystery and explain each one in one sentence.
  3. What is the difference between the "hell" Jesus descended into (inferos) and the hell of the damned (infernum)?
  4. List at least four of the seven lines of historical evidence for the Resurrection and explain why each one is important.
  5. What is anamnesis? Why is it not just "remembering" a past event?
  6. What does the C.S. Lewis trilemma say about Jesus' identity? Why can't Jesus be just a "good moral teacher mistaken about himself"?
  7. Name the five consequences of the Resurrection for our lives and briefly explain each one.
  8. What are the four parts of the Easter Vigil? Describe each one in two sentences.

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question with total honesty. Write by hand if you can.

1. Read 1 Corinthians 15:14. If Christ has not been raised, Paul says our faith is "in vain" —not weaker, not sadder, but empty. If someone asked you why you believe the Resurrection really happened, what would you say? Be specific.

Don't answer with "because the Church says so" or "because I was raised that way." Those are honest answers, but now go deeper. Give your own reason.

2. The women at the tomb expected to find a dead body. They found an angel who asked: "Why do you seek the living among the dead?" Where in your life are you "seeking the living among the dead" —looking for life, hope, or meaning in places where there is only death?

Be specific. Don't say "in sin." Name the concrete thing: a relationship, a habit, a fantasy, a resentment you refuse to let go.

3. Think of the worst suffering you have experienced or are experiencing right now. How does the Paschal Mystery speak to that suffering? Does it change anything, or does the Resurrection feel like a piece of theology that doesn't touch your actual pain?

Don't force a pious answer you don't feel. If the Resurrection doesn't console you right now, say so. That's where you start.

4. Paul says we were "buried with Christ by baptism into death, so that we too might walk in newness of life" (Rom 6:4). Do you walk in newness of life? What would have to change for that to be literally true of you? Name one concrete thing.

 

5. If Christ has truly risen and death has been defeated, what are you most afraid of? How does the Resurrection speak to that fear?

 

6. When the disciples heard the women's testimony that Jesus had risen, "these words seemed to them an idle tale, and they did not believe them" (Lk 24:11). Have you ever had a moment when you seriously doubted the Resurrection —not just had questions, but genuinely thought it might not be true? What triggered that doubt, and how did you come through it (if you did)?

If you're still in that doubt, be honest. Doubt is not the enemy of faith; indifference is. Faith that has never faced doubt is fragile. Faith that has faced doubt and survived is solid.

Practical Activities

A. The Paschal Timeline

Draw a timeline of the Paschal Triduum. Place the following events on it in order: Last Supper, Agony in Gethsemane, Arrest, Trial before the Sanhedrin, Trial before Pilate, Way of the Cross, Crucifixion, Death, Burial, Holy Saturday (great silence), Easter Vigil, Resurrection, Appearances, Ascension (forty days after). Use colors if you can. Keep this timeline for your personal prayer.

B. My Personal Kerygma

Write the apostolic kerygma from 1 Corinthians 15:3-8 in your own words, but make it personal. Instead of "Christ died for our sins," write "Christ died for my sins." Instead of "he appeared to Cephas," write "he has appeared in my life through..." Finish the sentence. This is your personal creed.

 

 

 

C. "The Stone" Exercise

Think of something in your life that feels like a sealed tomb: a situation that seems definitively dead, with no possible way out, no possible change. Write it down. Now read aloud John 11:39-44 (the raising of Lazarus) and Mark 16:4 ("the stone was rolled away"). The God who rolled away the stone of Lazarus' tomb and of Jesus' tomb can roll away your stone. Not necessarily in the way you expect. Not necessarily on your deadline. But he can. Pray with this conviction for one minute in silence.

D. The Race of Peter and John (Lk 24:12; Jn 20:3-8)

Read the account of how Peter and John run to the tomb. John arrives first but waits. Peter arrives and goes in first. John follows and "saw and believed." Now imagine you are John. Write two or three sentences describing what you saw, what you felt, what made you believe. What was it about the folded linen cloths that convinced you? (The fact that they were "lying there" and the face cloth "folded up in a place by itself" is highly significant: a grave robber doesn't stop to fold the linen.)

E. Witness Investigation

Investigate one of the historical witnesses of the Resurrection that we haven't discussed in depth. Research one of the following: Mary Magdalene (the "apostle to the apostles"), Cleopas and the road to Emmaus (Lk 24:13-35), the appearance to James (1 Cor 15:7), the appearance to 500+ (1 Cor 15:6). Write three things you discover about that witness. What do they add to the evidence?

F. Holy Week Commitment

Make a concrete commitment for this year's Holy Week:

  • I will participate in the Mass of the Lord's Supper on Holy Thursday: YES / NO
  • I will participate in the Good Friday liturgy: YES / NO
  • I will participate in the entire Easter Vigil (not just Sunday Mass): YES / NO
  • Something else I will do to live the Paschal Mystery this year: ___________

If you answered NO to any, write down why: ___________

Verse to Memorize

«If Christ has not been raised,
your faith is futile;
you are still in your sins.
But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead,
the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.»

— 1 Corinthians 15:17-18,20

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, Risen One,
who passed through death and came out on the other side,
who showed your wounds to Thomas and called him to touch,
who ate grilled fish with your friends on the shore of the lake:
give me the certainty that you are alive,
not as an idea, not as a memory,
but as a person who walks beside me every day.
Roll away the stones that seal my tombs:
my fear, my despair, my sin, my hopelessness.
Teach me to live as someone who has already risen,
and to bring resurrection wherever there is death.
Amen.

Print Workbook
